Trigger conditions that require a Reader Action will only detect its selected Reader Action:
Valid Card — Raised when a valid credential belonging to a user is detected.
Valid PIN — Raised when a valid PIN belonging to a User is detected.
Single Badge — Raised when a user presents their credential for the first time, or after ~10 seconds. Subsequent consecutive badges that are part of the same action are not detected.
2-Badge Action — Raised when a user presents their credential twice in a row, triggering a 2-Badge Action. Does not trigger again if a 3-Badge Action is done.
3-Badge Action — Raised when a user presents their credential three times in a row, triggering a 3-Badge Action
This trigger condition will be true while the current system date and time matches an active time block in the selected time period.
This trigger condition will be true while the current system date and time does not match any active time blocks in the selected time period.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected output is on. Note that if the Invert Output State option is used for a Hardware Output, this trigger is based on the internal system state, not the physical relay state.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected output is off. Note that if the Invert Output State option is used for a Hardware Output, this trigger is based on the internal system state, not the physical relay state.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected area is armed.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected area is disarmed.
This trigger condition will be true whenever entry delay is started by a user entering the selected area while it is armed, triggering an input that is assigned the
process group. The trigger condition will remain true for the duration of the entry delay which is configured in that area or until the area is disarmed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever a user arms an area and triggers the exit delay which defines the amount of time they have to exit the area. The trigger condition will remain true for the duration of the exit delay which is configured in that area. Note that exit delay is only triggered on LCD Terminal arming or using the web interface option
This trigger condition will be true whenever an alarm is triggered in the selected area, and will stay true until the area is disarmed.

This trigger condition will be true as long as the area has at least one active alarm of the chosen Filter to Alarm Type that has not yet restored. Note that this only relates to active alarms, for example, an intruder detector detecting motion in a disarmed area would not trigger this condition.

This trigger condition will be true whenever there is at least one active input in the selected area, staying true until there are no active inputs in the area. This occurs regardless of the area arm state. If triggering actions on each input activation is required, the new activation may be detected with the condition 'Detecting New Input Activity (Pulsed)' instead.

This trigger condition will pulse whenever an input in the selected area becomes active. This occurs regardless of the area arm state. This triggers for every individual input activation in the area even when another input is still currently active.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Full Area mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Perimeter Mode mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.

This trigger condition will be true if the selected area has been configured with multi-mode arming and has been armed into the Night Mode mode, turning false when no longer armed or armed in a different mode.
This trigger condition will be true while the area is in the 'Arm Warning' state, defined by the option

. The arm warning phase occurs prior to an area automatically arming by the system, allowing users time to exit the area or cancel the arm. The auto-arming can occur because of area inactivity, scheduling, or defer area arming. This condition remains true until the warning time has elapsed and the area arms, or the arm warning phase is cancelled.
This trigger condition will be true for a few seconds after a successful area arm attempt.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever the reporting process successfully sends out any messages for closings in this area. Note that the area's
must be configured to report closings for this trigger condition to be possible. This trigger can be used to show a visual or audible response that the close event was successfully reported.
This trigger condition will be true for a few seconds after an unsuccessful area arm attempt.

This trigger condition will be true if an Area Walk Test has been started through the web interface for the selected area, and will become false when the Area Walk Test has been cancelled or is successfully completed. Walk test mode is automatically cancelled if the area is armed while the test is running.

This trigger condition will be true if the number of users in the area meets the chosen comparison to the Number of Users value, and will turn back to false if it no longer meets this condition after the number of users in the area changes again.
This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently locked.
This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently open.
This trigger condition will be true while the door is currently closed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever the door is placed into the Lockout state, preventing users being granted access to the door. This trigger condition will last until the lockout is cleared and the door has reverted back to its normal behaviour.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is opened without the door being unlocked first, causing the door to enter the 'forced' state. This trigger condition will stay true until the door closes or is unlocked.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is in the 'Held Open Warning' phase. This occurs when the door locks while still open, and remains that way for the Held (DOTL) Time, entering a warning phase for Held (DOTL) Warning Time. This condition allows local responses to close the door before it is officially held open too long and alarm events are reported, etc. If the door closes, the door is unlocked again, or if that warning time expires and the door becomes 'Held Open', this trigger condition ends.

This trigger condition will be true when the door is in the 'Held Open Too Long' phase, following a door remaining open after locking for the Held (DOTL) Time and Held (DOTL) Warning Time. The trigger condition will remain true until the door is closed or it is unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true when any readers linked to the Door have detected reader tampering, where it will remain true until the tampering has been resolved.

This trigger condition will pulse whenever a user is denied access for the selected door. This could be due to insufficient permissions or the door being in a Locked Out state. Note that this applies for known and unknown users requesting access to the door. Unknown credentials or inactive credentials do trigger this condition.
This trigger condition will be true whenever the wireless door reports a Low Battery state to the Inception system.
This trigger condition will be true when communication is lost between the wireless lock and the hub, or the hub and the module.

This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor is secured, which requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. When the lift floor is Locked Out it is also Secured.
This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor is in the Free Access state.
This trigger condition will be true while the lift floor has been locked out and will only turn false when this state has been overridden.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is secured.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true if the storage unit is still unlocked after a time period that can be defined by the
option in the block that the unit is associated with. This trigger condition will become false again after the unit is locked again.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is currently open.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is currently closed.

This trigger condition will be true whenever an alarm is triggered for the selected storage unit, which occurs when there is an active input within a secured unit. This state will remain true until all inputs in that unit are no longer active or if the unit unlocks or is marked as vacant.

This trigger condition will be true whenever tamper is triggered for the selected storage unit, which occurs when a detector within the Unit is tampered with. This state will remain true until all inputs in that unit are no longer tampered.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the storage unit has been marked vacant, and will only turn false again if the vacancy has been cleared.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected storage unit is in the secure pending state, due to it having one or more unsealed inputs.
This trigger condition will be true while all storage units in the chosen storage block are currently secure.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are currently unlocked.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are currently in the state of being unlocked too long after a time period defined by the
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block are currently in alarm.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block are currently in tamper.
This trigger condition will be true while there are one or more storage units in the chosen storage block that are pending secure due to unsealed inputs.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input is secure.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input is active.
This trigger condition will be true as long as the chosen input has been isolated, and will only turn false when de-isolated.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever a received message from the connection matches the defined message value of this condition.

This trigger condition is true while the selected connection is enabled and is able to use the configured details to retain a connection. Note that other states such as Connecting or Disabled are not considered to be Connected.
This trigger condition is true while the selected connection has been enabled but is Disconnected, has failed to connect with the given configuration, or is in the process of connecting.
Detecting Reader Action by Any User (Pulsed)
This trigger condition will pulse whenever any user performs the selected Reader Action at the selected reader.
Detecting Reader Action by Specific User (Pulsed)
This trigger condition will pulse whenever the selected User performs the selected Reader Action at the chosen reader.
Detecting Reader Action by User with Permission Group (Pulsed)

This trigger condition will pulse whenever any user with the given Permission Group performs the selected Reader Action at the selected reader. This trigger allows more generic conditions to be defined, such as triggering if any Manager badges. It also allows the condition to remain up to date as users are added to or removed from the system.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever an invalid or unknown card has been detected at the selected reader.
This trigger condition will pulse whenever an invalid PIN has been entered at the selected reader.
This trigger condition will be true while the selected Hardware Module is offline. This condition applies to any expansion module.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected cabinet tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion module with a dedicated Cabinet Tamper input.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected internal siren tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to expansion modules with dedicated Internal Siren drivers.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected external siren tampering, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to expansion modules with dedicated External Siren drivers.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected that the AC Power is missing, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a low battery, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a missing battery, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a shorted wire across power output terminals, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected a failed power supply, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the latest battery test on the selected Hardware Module has failed, remaining true until another successful battery test (automatic or manual) is completed. This condition can apply to the Controller or any expansion modules that is monitoring a Smart Power Supply.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected that module substitution has occurred, remaining true while a module substitution is still being detected. This condition can apply to any expansion module.
This trigger condition will pulse when a failed PIN attempt has been detected by the hardware module. This condition can apply only to Terminal expansion modules.

This trigger condition will pulse when user duress has been detected by the hardware module. This condition can apply to Terminal expansion modules or SLAMs with keypad readers attached. The event is raised if the Duress User PIN is entered at a keypad reader or a terminal, or if [HELP] [HELP] [HELP] is pressed at a Terminal module.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected RF interference or jamming, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply only to RF Input expansion modules.

This trigger condition will be true when the selected Hardware Module has detected tampering on a door reader, remaining true while the issue persists. This condition can apply to the Controller or any door expansion modules.
This trigger condition will be true if the selected counter's value meets the chosen comparison to the counter value option, and will turn back to false if it no longer meets this condition.
This trigger condition will be true if the selected Integrated Connection encounters a connection error, and will turn back to false when this connection error is resolved.

This trigger condition will pulse whenever a {0} event is detected on the selected Integrated Device

The area (partition) reporting id used when reporting system health events like System Resets, Cabinet Tamper, Module Offline, AC Fail etc. This should be unique across all other area reporting ids in the system. This has the same function as the 'system area' within Concept 4000 and Integriti systems
The Repeat Alarm Event Limit or

is a feature designed to limit the number of repeated input activations or module health issues that are sent to a monitoring station. Often, this option is used in case a detector device becomes faulty and begins repeatedly activating, preventing repeated false signals from being sent through. It can also be used to stop an intruder from intentionally activating an input many times and masking their movements in the flood of input events from that one input.
If the limit is set to zero, then the feature is disabled and no limit is applied.

In addition to input events, module health issues are also tracked and are affected by the repeat event limit. For module events, this can prevent events like Low Battery from sending repeatedly if the battery capacity is on the edge of detection.

The system tracks individual input events and module health events towards the repeat event limit, identifying each event separately. In this case, repeated alarm events from an input can trigger swinger shutdown and stopping future alarm events from that input from being sent, but if a tamper event is then detected on that input then it will still be reported. Similarly, a module's low battery event could be limited, but a cabinet tamper or AC fail event from that module is different and hence tracked and limited separately and will still be sent through.

Once the event limit is reached for an input or module health event, a separate event is sent to the monitoring station for this input or module to identify this. All new activations for that input or module health issue will then be discarded and will continue until an area disarm. For inputs, the limit is reset when the area that includes the input is disarmed. For module health events, as there is no area that they belong to, any area disarm will reset the limits.
Prevents the Technician On Site event from being reported. Some monitoring stations don't handle the event well, so the event can be disabled if it is causing issues.

- An area arm attempt by a user that fails due to unsealed inputs will result in a Failed to Close event being reported.

The entry delay is the duration of time that the user has to disarm an area without generating alarms. The entry delay is started when a user enters an armed area and triggers an input that is assigned the
process group. It is recommended that the entry delay duration be long enough for a user to reach a keypad and disarm an area.
The exit delay is the duration of time that the user has to exit the area after arming the area. Inputs that are assigned the
process groups form an exit path and, if triggered, will not generate an area alarm during the exit delay.
It is recommended that the exit delay duration be long enough for a user to successfully exit the building.
The exit delay is only triggered when an area is armed from an LCD Terminal or the web interface via the
Temporary disarm is a feature that allows a user to disarm an area, with the area automatically re-arming at the end of the

time. Temporary disarming, or defer arm, is often used to ensure that an area is not accidentally left disarmed when the user leaves the site. The temporary disarm feature is commonly used for users who access an area for a short period of time, such as a cleaner, or by someone who is accessing the area after hours.
If the temporary disarm feature is being used in the area, it is recommended to use the
option below, as this alerts users that the area will re-arm soon. This option provides the user with time to either leave the area or disarm the area a second time.

A user does not need to perform a special action to temporarily disarm an area, instead, the decision for whether an area is disarmed normally or temporarily disarmed is based on the user's permissions. To allow a user to disarm an area temporarily, assign to the user permission to control the area with the

. When the user requests to disarm the area, the user is first checked to see if they have permission to disarm the area normally. If not, they are then checked to see if they have permission to temporarily disarm the area. This allows normal disarm permissions to take precedence. This occurs with any disarm request from the user, be it from a terminal, the web interface, card swipe or remote fob button press.
Temporary disarm can be used with the

option below to ensure the area is not left disarmed overnight. A user could be given permission to disarm the area normally throughout the day, but then only given permission to temporarily disarm the area at night. If the user stays back after the auto-arm scheduled time, when the user cancels the automatic arm the area will only be temporarily disarmed, ensuring that once they do leave, the area will arm in case they forget.

This prevents the user from needing to repeatedly disarm the area each time the delay time was reached, while still allowing them to stay longer than the time if required on rare occasions by disarming again.

Provides an additional period of time before an area automatically arms to allow users to exit or manually disarm the area. The auto-arming of an area can occur because of area inactivity, scheduling or defer area arming. The auto-arm warn time can be used to generate an audible warning tone on sirens, siren outputs, readers and keypads. This allows the user time to either leave the area, or disarm the area to cancel the arm.

If enabled, this area can be armed into several different modes. This replaces the basic Arm mode, allowing the area to be armed in Full (Away), Perimeter (Stay) or Night (Instant) modes. These modes are commonly used in home scenarios, where the exterior of the building should be armed while people are inside. As with the standard arm mode, only the intruder related input types are affected. Inputs like Smoke and Duress will be monitored regardless of the arm mode.
The arm modes have the following behaviour:
- Exactly the same behaviour as the standard Arm mode. The whole area is armed with Exit Delay if required. Intended for use when everyone is leaving the area.
- Arms only the perimeter of the area. Intended for use when people will remain in the area, securing only the perimeter of the building. Only the perimeter inputs are monitored (specified in the
option below). If a Primary input activates, entry delay will begin (e.g someone opens a door).
- Intended for use when people will remain in the area, but no one is expected to enter or leave. The perimeter inputs and interior night inputs are monitored (specified in the

option below). If any perimeter input activates (including Primary inputs), the alarm will activate immediately. This allows warning if someone breaks into the front door while everyone is asleep for example. Optionally, additional interior night inputs can be specified to monitor more of the area in this mode. These interior inputs are treated as Primary inputs, triggering entry delay (and hence not the sirens immediately) if someone activates those inputs.

The pulse count determines the number of input alarm activations required before an area alarm is triggered. Activations are counted from both the same input triggering multiple times or from different inputs within the area, and activations must occur within the
time to trigger the alarm. Once an alarm has been triggered, pulse counting is stopped and each subsequent activation is processed normally.

Input pulse counting is often used to help reduce false alarms from being reported, allowing once-off activations to be ignored while repeat activations are processed. False alarms can occur for a wide variety of reasons, with some common examples being pets moving around or wind from a climate control system or a storm blowing items and triggering motion detection or vibration sensors.

Pulse counting only affects alarm events from Primary, Handover or Instant Alarm inputs. Other input type events such as Duress, Smoke Detection, Medical or Emergency will all be processed normally. Similarly, any input tamper events will also be processed normally.

When choosing a value for the pulse count it is important to consider the environment this area is protecting. A high-security environment would require a lower pulse count in order to trigger sooner, while a location with pets or a harsh environment may require a higher count to filter out occasional activations.
The window of time where a number of input alarm activations must occur before an alarm is reported. The number of activations is configured in

In addition to pulse counting, an area alarm will also be triggered if an activation is detected that remains active for longer than the pulse duration without restoring. This prevents pulse counting from being bypassed by an input activating but never restoring.

One or more inputs can be selected that are used to cut an active exit delay short. These are often used to prevent long exit delays from running for the full duration when the user has already left the area.

Commonly, the primary input for the area or the exit door reed switch or tongue sense is used, or a button on the outside of the building to ensure that the user has left the area before cutting exit delay short. The input(s) specified do not need to be inputs that are monitored by the area, any inputs in the system can be used.

To activate the feature, any of the inputs specified need to transition from an active state to a secure state. A small delay is built in when processing the input that caused the exit delay to complete to prevent false alarms. This is to ensure that the input has settled incase, for example, the input is a door reed switch or tongue sense that could bounce briefly when the door closes.
Ignoring of module health issues for this area affects two main features in the system:

- By default, if a module indicates a tamper issue (such as siren or cabinet tampers), all areas in the system will generate an alarm response by sounding sirens and activating strobes. This may be undesirable on non-security areas as they would need to be disarmed. Enabling of this option causes this area to be excluded from the automatic tamper response.
Verify System Health on Area Arm

page causes area arm attempts to check for any module health issues or alarms comms issues in the system. These are automatically overridden if the system arms the area, however if a user arms the area the arm attempt can fail. If enabled, inputs on the exit path will auto-isolate after the exit delay ends. If the input returns to a secure state or the area is disarmed, the isolated input will automatically de-isolate. The inputs that were late to secure would also automatically de-isolate once secure.

Note, this option should only be used after careful consideration, as it is a trade off between convenience for security and may be undesirable. This checkbox enables and disables the automated action. This can be used to temporarily disable the action.

The trigger condition that determines when this automated action is true or false. When the trigger condition changes from false to true, a set of actions can be automatically executed. Similarly, when the trigger condition changes from true to false, a different set of actions can be automatically executed.

The trigger condition can use the state of any area, door, input, output or time period and will create a trigger statement that is either true or false. For example, a trigger statement could be that the
Door Front Door is not Locked

. Groups can also be created, linking multiple statements or even other groups together. All of these statements and groups will combine together and the trigger condition will still be either true or false.
Groups can be processed in one of two ways:
True when ALL of the following are true
- This group is true when ALL of the trigger statements and groups in this group are true, otherwise it is false.
True when ANY of the following are true
- This group is true when ANY one of the trigger statements and groups in this group are true, otherwise it is false.

As the areas, doors, inputs, outputs and time periods change state in the system, the trigger condition is continually tested and when it changes from true to false or false to true, the correct set of actions are executed.
This allows both simple and complex automatic responses to be created, potentially executing a response only if multiple items in the system are in the correct state.

Determines what action or actions to perform when the trigger condition changes from false to true. These actions are edge-based and allow for the control of many things (outputs, areas, doors, inputs, readers, etc).

Determines what action or actions to perform when the trigger condition changes from true to false. These actions are edge-based and allow for the control of many things (outputs, areas, doors, inputs, readers, etc).

Chooses the method of connection that this Connection item should utilise. For a more detailed explanation of how the different Connection Types work, please see the Connections page description at the top of this page.

- The Connection sends and receives data over a specified serial port. Serial ports can be used with USB to Serial converters and multiple ports can be achieved with a USB Hub. This connection type is best suited for when bidirectional communication is required between Inception and an external system.

- The Connection acts as a TCP client and attempts to open a connection to a specified IP adress and port. This connection type is best suited for instances where the Inception device is the one responsible for initiating the connection and sending messages to an external system.

- The Connection acts as a TCP server and listens for incoming connections on a specified port. This connection type is best suited for instances where the external system is the one responsible for initiating the connection to the Inception device and sending messages.
- The Connection sends HTTP requests to a server at a specified URL/IP/hostname, and the
sent through the connection are treated as the end of the URL and/or the query string. For example: with the Connection's URL set to
results in the Connection sending a GET request to the URL

. HTTP Connections can only send messages, no messages can be received. HTTP Connections can optionally use Basic or Digest authentication for APIs that require valid user credentials. HTTP Send Connections cannot be used to receive messages, a separate HTTP (Receive) connection must be created to do this.

- The Connection receives HTTP requests made to a specific endpoint on the Inception web server (/receive_http_connection), with a URL query parameter (key) to identify which connection the message is intended for by matching on the Key String field. HTTP Receive Connections can receive messages either from GET requests containing text in the

to send a message to a connection with a Key string of example), or POST requests containing text in the request body. HTTP Receive connections can optionally require a username and password for HTTP Basic authentication (Digest authentication is not supported for receiving).

HTTP Connections can optionally use an authentication method to communicate with other systems' HTTP APIs that require valid user credentials in order to perform actions.

Enables anti-passback logic on this door. Anti-passback adds additional validation of user access requests that check whether the user is already in the area they are trying to access. This uses the last known location of the user and is intended to prevent tailgating, moving throughout a building without following access control proceedures or passing a card back to allow someone else access to the area. Note that the user still requires permission to access the door.

If a user always presents their access credential when accessing doors, even if a colleague is holding the door open for them, then the user's location will always be accurate and they will never be stopped by anti-passback. Violations only occur when the user ends up in a different area than what the system knows. This could be by changing areas without presenting their credential, or presenting their credential at a door but changing their mind and not going through the door for example.

Anti-passback is commonly used on sites whose policies rely on accurate user locations. This could be for time on site reports, accurate muster reports for OH&S compliance and safety, or secure locations in the building. It can also help protect resources such as a carpark, preventing access into the carpark, a card being passed back and another driver entering with the same credential.
It is recommended that doors with anti-passback logic enabled on them have a credential reader on both sides of the door to allow proper location updating.

The below configuration options can determine when the anti-passback rules apply, additional rules on what can cause a violation and how the system responds when a violation occurs. If access is denied due to the violation, forgiveness or

The anti-passback logic for this door can optionally be limited to specific times of the day. This can allow, for example, the anti-passback rules to be enforced during the day while others are present to forgive violations but to not be applied at night when a user could become locked in
If a time period is selected, the anti-passback rules will only apply when the time period is active. Otherwise, anti-passback rules will always apply.

By default, anti-passback logic only checks that the user is not currently in the area they are trying to access. If enabled, the system will also check that the user is located in the area they are exiting from. If this option is used, credential readers on both sides of the doors throughout the site are strongly recommended so user locations are correctly updated.
This allows for more advanced antipassback logic, ensuring that users always use their access credentials to move around the building.

Site security is improved with this option as a duplicated card will not work if the real owner is already on site. Similarly, access to the site through correct entry and exit points can be enforced as internal door access would not work if the users current location is outside.
This type of anti-passback does not need to be used on all doors in the system. It could be limited, for example, to a section of the site where strict control of access is required.
For users familiar with other Inner Range products, this option is similar to the
logic from Concept 4000 systems or the

When an anti-passback violation occurs a review event will be automatically logged, a system message (a blue message on the System Overview page requiring acknowledgement) will be created and optionally notifications to users can be sent.
This option determines whether the user will be denied access to the door or not due to the violation. This functionality is the difference between

If access is not denied, the user is allowed through the door even though a violation occured. However, an administrator could follow up this violation at a future time for example. This option may be used on a site to help introduce and train the users about anti-passback, generating violations without delaying the users or affecting productivity.

If enabled, the user will be denied access through the door when a violation occurs. In this case, the user will need to be granted forgiveness or amnesty by an administrator which gives them one free pass through an anti-passback door to correct their user location. Alternatively, a user could correct their location by using a non anti-passback door or with someone else opening the door for them. The
option below will also become available.

response is enabled above, the user will be denied access to the door if they violate anti-passback. To access the door, someone needs to open the door for them or they need to contact a system administrator to forgive the violation which could all take time.

This option allows a configurable amount of time to be set where forgiveness will automatically be given to the user after a violation occurs. This allows annoyance for the user who caused the violation, being stopped for 20 seconds for example, without being too inconvenient like having to report to reception or contact someone. The user may learn to always use the access control readers and other users don't need to continuously forgive violations as they occur.

Longer durations of minutes to hours could be chosen to ensure a violation is forgiven if it remains for too long. An example could be overnight, where the user violated anti-passback when leaving the building. A colleague may have let them out but the violation remains. With a long auto forgiveness, this violation will be cleared overnight and the user will be able to access the building the next morning.

- The input maintains its default behaviour according to its defined EOL configuration. This is the default value set for all newly created inputs.

-  If selected, the input's alarm/active state is swapped with the sealed/inactive state. This is used when a normally-open detection device, such as a smoke detector, is wired to the system. A normally-open device reverses the End of Line resistance and will appear sealed when in alarm and in alarm when sealed.
Note: This can be used for detectors, switches and buttons.

- If selected, the input's alarm/active state is swapped with the tamper state. This option is intended for sites where the majority of inputs follow the standard EOL logic, however some sensors (fire sensors, etc.) behave differently and require that their alarm and tamper state are flipped.

If enabled, if this input detects a Mask state based on End of Line resistance values, the system will automatically translate this to an Alarm state. This option is primarily used when two physical detectors are linked together into the one input, for example if two door reed switches on a double-door are wired into the one input. In this case, the addition of End of Line resistance from two alarm conditions could be incorrectly treated as Mask instead of Alarm.

If enabled, allows this lift car to use alternative access programming, if the assigned schedule is active. This allows some of the access options for a lift car to change based on the time of day. Note that if enabled, the

During the day, a user could use their Card or PIN to access a lift car for convenience, however after hours, the site could require that both Card and PIN are used for higher security. Similarly, access modes could differ based on weekday vs weekends and so on.

For alternate access mode to be used, a schedule must be configured. The schedule can consist of one or more trigger conditions that evaluate to True or False (e.g. when a Time Period is Active, or when an Area is Armed), which are processed in combination to calculate whether the schedule should be active or not. The schedule logic can process the conditions such that the alternate mode schedule is only active when ALL of the conditions are true, or active when ANY of the conditions are true. When the schedule's state is active, the alternate set of options below will be applied, overriding the default programming for the lift car.

This is another way to limit access to a floor, where a user is normally allowed to access the floor when they present their credential, but only once an authorized user has accessed the area and disarmed it first.

This checkbox enables and disables the notifier from monitoring system events. This can be used to temporarily disable the processing without needing to delete or edit the programming.

Product Variant disables all notifiers in the system by default, in order to prevent cases such as restoring a customer's database backup on a test Inception for diagnosis and having undesired notifications being automatically sent out. This checkbox may be enabled in combination with the
checkbox in order to explicitly enable a Notifier on a IRSupport system.

Monitoring of events for this notifier can optionally be limited to specific times of the day. Limiting event monitoring can, for example, allow events to be sent at only certain times of the day, or can allow events to be sent to specific people at certain times of the day and to different people at other times.
If a time period is selected, monitoring of events for this notifier will only occur when the time period is active. Otherwise, this notifier will always be monitoring for events to send.

option is enabled, this setting will determine how long Inception will attempt to resend failed push notifications generated by this Notifier (e.g. due to network failure). To prevent too many emails from being sent and potentially filling an inbox or having the configured SMTP server marked as a spam server, Inception will attempt to group messages together if they occur close together. Messages will be grouped only if they are being sent to the same recipient address and have the same subject. Any messages that are grouped will include the date and time they occurred within the message.

When an email is to be sent, Inception will wait 15 seconds in case any other events occur for the same recipient address and subject and will group them into a single email message. Allows users to be notified if any module is detected as being potentially substituted.

During commissioning, when all hardware modules are configured and online, a LAN secure process is performed to identify all present modules. After that, if a module connects that was not online when the LAN secure process was performed, they are considered to be substituted. This could be part of maintenance such as replacing a damaged module, or could be compromising the site security by replacing an existing module with a fake version.
If a module substitution error occurs, a technician is needed to inspect the hardware and LAN to ensure the security of the system before performing a new LAN secure process.
Allows users to be notified if an LCD Terminal triggers its PIN Lockout mode. This could indicate that someone is attempting to guess a user's PIN to gain access to the system.

Allows users to be notified when any health issues related to a module are overridden or isolated. Overriding of health issues causes the system to temporarily stop monitoring the module, often allowing areas to be armed.

Temporary isolation occurs if the health issue was present when an area was armed, allowing the arm to succeed. This isolation will last until the area is disarmed. Users can also permenantly isolate modules from reporting issues if there is an ongoing fault with the module, removing the need to temporarily isolate the health issue each time the area is armed.

Allows users to be notified when an RF Remote assigned to a user reports a low battery event. The notification will include the name of the user who owns the remote.

Allows users to be notified when a user successfully logs into the Inception web interface. The notification will include the name of the user who logged on.

Allows users to be notified if Inception triggers lockout mode on the web interface. This could indicate that someone is attempting to guess a user's web login credentials to gain access to the system.

Web interface lockout is triggered when multiple failed web login attempts occur in a row. The number of attempts required to trigger web interface lockout is three, preventing any users from logging in for a short period of time. If the failed login attempts continue, the amount of time that the web interface is locked out for will increase, reducing the effectiveness of a brute-force attack.
Allows users to be notified whenever service mode is enabled or disabled in the system. The message will include which user enabled or disabled service mode.

Service mode allows features like sirens and alarm reporting to be temporarily disabled to allow maintenance of the system without having to modify programming, however if done without permission it could compromise the security of the system.
Allows users to be notified if an alarm reporting path to an alarm monitoring station has broken or has been restored.

Reporting path failures are typically also known by the monitoring station, as the reporting paths are polled. Any alarms that occur while the path is down will be queued up to be sent once the connection restores itself.

If set, the reader lockout functionality for this reader will be disabled.

The reader lockout feature helps to protect the system from brute force attacks where multiple access cards or PIN numbers are attempted in order to impersonate a user. If multiple access cards or PIN credentials are presented at a reader that do not match a user, reader lockout is triggered. Only credentials that do not match a user are counted, attempts that are denied due to permissions do not count.

Three failed credential attempts in a row trigger a lockout on this reader which will last for 90 seconds. Once triggered, any credentials presented to the reader will be ignored until the lockout time expires. After the lockout time has expired, repeat invalid credentials will cause the 90 second lockout time to restart.

Editing and saving of a User within the system resets the invalid credential count for all readers and clears reader lockout if it has been triggered. This allows cards to be assigned to users by swiping an unknown card at a reader, then assigning that unknown card swipe to a user, without worrying about triggering reader lockout.

Otherwise, the key will use the PIN entered to try to control the area instead of requesting access to the door. How the area is controlled will depend on the current state of the area. If enabled, the SIFER/OSDP reader will provide feedback for the following states:
-  If the door is held open for too long the reader will make a beeping sound until the door reed or tongue sense are sealed. DOTL functionality is configured in the
- When a user without permission to access the door badges a card, the SIFER reader will make a single beep and the Big LED will flash red.
- When the door is unlocked by any method the SIFER reader will make a single beep and the Big LED will flash green.
If enabled, the SIFER/OSDP reader will provide feedback for the area located on the same side of the door as the reader. Feedback is provided for the following area states:
- When the area is successfully armed, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Arm Success tone and the Big LED will flash green.
- When the area fails to arm, either due to permissions or unsealed inputs, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Arm Failure tone and the Big LED will flash red.
- When there is an active alarm in the area, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Alarm tone and the Small LED will fast flash red until the area is disarmed.
- When the area's entry delay mode is active, the SIFER reader will sound the Entry Delay tone and the Small LED will flash green for the duration of the entry delay.
- When the area's exit delay mode is active, the SIFER reader will sound the Exit Delay tone and the Small LED will flash green for the duration of the exit delay.

- When the area is about to automatically arm, the SIFER reader will sound the Area Auto Arm Warning tone and the Small LED will flash orange until the area is automatically armed or manually disarmed.

Note: The states above are prioritised by the system in the order they are listed. If the SIFER reader is configured to provide area state feedback for the areas on both sides of the door, both areas are tested and the area state with the higher priority will be provided.

The type of report to generate.

- Generates a report which shows a list of events where Users have requested access through Doors or access to Lift Cars, including the time of the access event and whether the access was granted or denied.  Optionally filterable by Users, Doors or Lift Cars, and Access Granted/Denied Status.

- Generates a report which shows a list of events where Users or the System have requested to arm or disarm Areas. Optionally filterable by Areas, Users, Arm/Disarm events, User/System events, and Successful/Failed events.

- Generates a report which shows the list of item permissions and Permission Groups that each User has been assigned. Has the option of generating different levels of detail for reports, depending on how much information is required. Optionally filterable by Users and Items.

- Generates a report which shows the list of all Users that have permission to interact with each item. Similar to the User Permissions Summary, except the report is grouped by the item. Optionally filterable by Users and Items.
- Generates a report which shows a list of Users along with the Area that they are currently in. Optionally filterable by Areas.
- Generates a report which shows all Users who have not interacted with the system (i.e. accessed a Door, armed an Area) for a given period of time (default is 1 month).

- Generates a report containing detailed summaries of Users in the system. The details shown for each user include (if applicable) the User's Name, Notes, LCD Terminal Profile, Email Address, Assigned Cards & Remote Fobs, and more. Optionally filterable by Users, and Users who are Expired/Expiring Soon.

- Generates a report which shows a list of time periods that Users have been on site for, based on access events from the specified Read-In/Read-Out Readers. Optionally filterable by Users. This type of report is recommended if the site has strict Badge-in, Badge-out policies when entering and leaving site. Report will show the amount of time between each badge in and each badge out, listing per-user each site visit over the report timeframe. Read-In and Read-Out readers must be specified.

- Generates a report which shows a list of Users have been on site each day and for how long, based on their first and last reader badge events for the day (a single day is treated as being from 12am-11:59pm). Optionally filterable by Users and Doors. This type of report is recommended if Users are only on site during regular daytime hours (i.e. no night shifts going past midnight), and if your site don't have strictly defined in/out readers.

- Generates a report which shows a list of events where Users or the System have requested to secure or unlock Storage Units. Optionally filterable by Storage Units, Users, Secure/Unlock events, User/System events, Successful/Failed events, and whether Vacancy Control events should be included.
- Generates a report which shows a list of events raised by an Integrated Connection or its Integrated Devices. Optionally filterable by Integrated Devices, as well as specific event types.
Chooses the file format of the report.

- Generates the report as a single PDF document. NOTE: This option may be unable to handle creating very large reports (e.g. User Permissions Summary Reports of 500+ pages) due to system limitations; add filters to the data to reduce the report size or choose one of the other file formats if you need to create a very large report.
- Generates the report as a single HTML document.
- Generates the report as a series of individual PDF documents of approximately 150 pages each at maximum, packed together for downloading in a single ZIP file.

- Generates the report as a comma-separated value (CSV) text file, ideal for manipulation or processing in external applications, e.g. spreadsheet software. NOTE: CSV reports are text-only and therefore lack the formatting of the other file type options; if the report's readability is important, consider using one of the PDF or HTML format options instead.

- The report shows a flattened view of all the items a user has access to through both direct assignment and Permission Groups, and also the conditions which determine when these permissions are available (e.g. Always, only during Working Hours). This type of report is best suited for getting a clear view of all the possible actions that Users can perform in the system.

This type of report is best suited for getting a highly detailed, in-depth look at all of the possible actions that Users can perform in the system, as well as the reason(s) why these permissions are available to them.

This report does not flatten out Permission Groups and shows only the direct assignments. This type of report is best suited for getting a simple overview of what items each User has been directly assigned permission to in the system, or seeing which Users have been assigned to which Permission Groups.

This setting determines the response to the sealing of all inputs of a storage unit that is in Alarm.
By leaving this option disabled, the unit will remain in Alarm until a

action occurs for the unit, latching the alarm state. Clearing the alarm could be by the units tenant presenting their credential at the Block entry reader to unlock it, or by a User controlling the unit via the
page on the Inception web interface. This allows users to easily see which unit triggered the alarm, and allows monitoring stations to know when the alarm event has actually been restored.

If the option is enabled, the Unit alarm state will be cleared as soon as all inputs within the secure unit are sealed or when the unit is unlocked. This allows the unit alarm state to follow the state of its inputs, but has the consequence that if a unit only has a door sensor then someone could open a secure unit door, enter, then close it straight away, and the unit would no longer be in alarm.
For unmanned storage unit facilities, it is recommended that this option is disabled and that the area configured to monitor the storage units alarm states has the
Cancel Alarm on All Inputs Secure

option enabled. This will allow sirens and alarms to occur if someone accesses a secure storage unit, but if it was an accident, then the units tenant can unlock the unit and the alarm can be cancelled.

Select calendar exceptions to apply to this Time Period.
These exceptions are days/times in which this time period will be inactive. These are defined in the Calendars menu.
Defines the start and ends times of the time period, for each day of the week. During these times and on the days selected the time period will be valid/active.

If the start time is earlier than the end time (e.g 9am -> 5pm), then the time period will be valid during those times on the day specified. Alternatively, if the start and end time are the same (5pm -> 5pm) or the end time is earlier than the start (10pm -> 2am), then the valid block will start on the day specified and end on the next day. This allows an easy way to specify time blocks spanning midnight. This means, for exmaple, 10pm -> 2am on a Wednesday will be valid from 10pm to midnight on Wednesdays, and valid from midnight to 2am on Thursdays. Similarly, if the start and end times match, 5pm -> 5pm on Wednesday will be valid for 24 hours from 5pm Wednesday to 5pm Thursday.
For the time period to be valid for an entire day, 12am -> 12am can be configured, or the
To add additional time blocks, click the
button, this will open a window where the times and days can be selected.

A list of exceptions which apply on the days when their associated calendar is active (higher priorities will override lower ones). A time period that contains calendar exceptions will have its active or inactive state overridden by the calendar exception on the days for which it’s configured.

Calendar exceptions are used when the same time change for a day should be repeated on multiple days throughout the year. The calendar can be updated, which could affect multiple Time Periods in a consistent way. A common use case example for calendar exceptions are extended trading hour days, where trading hours change consistently on many days throughout the year. The calendar in that case could apply to multiple time periods, like working hours, automatic door times and automatic arm times, each reacting to the same calendar. A user can then update the calendar throughout the year and the system reacts correctly.
Start and end times for the exception are programmed in the same way as the

option above, where a start time that is earlier than the end time (e.g 9am -> 5pm) affects a single day, but the same start and end time (5pm -> 5pm) or an earlier end time (10pm -> 2am) will span midnight and affect the following day too. More details can be seen in the tooltip of the

A list of individual time and date based exceptions which override the list of weekly recurring times (higher priorities will override lower ones). Exceptions behave in the same manner as calendar exceptions, overriding the active or inactive state of the time period's weekly recurring times, however these exceptions only exist within this particular time period.

Commonly used to add a temporary specific change to the time period's normal behaviour without changing the recurring times, which could also be added in advance. For example, shutting down for a maintenance period or a brief period of extended trading hours.

The security PIN is a unique code that allows a user to control areas and outputs from an LCD terminal, or access a door from a Wiegand keypad reader.

The security PIN can also be used to log onto the web interface instead of using a password. The security PIN will not be visible once the user is saved, if the user has forgotten their security PIN, you may press the “Send PIN” button. This will open a new window which allows an email with the Security PIN and basic details to be sent to the user. Otherwise, another user with higher privileges (such as an installer) with “Reveal PIN” permissions may use this button to reveal and view the PIN.
Security PINs must be unique across users, you will be unable to save changes to a user if a duplicate pin is entered. To generate a unique pin for a user click the

If enabled, this user's cards will be permantly stored in the internal card caches of any Standard LAN Access Modules (SLAMs) on the module LAN.

The SLAM is capable of holding 2000 cards in an internal cache, tracking whether access was granted the last time it was presented at a reader and remembering this per-door controlled by the SLAM. If communications between the SLAM and the Inception system are lost, the SLAM can use this card cache to determine if a user's card should be allowed through the door, allowing offline behaviour.

Cards are automatically removed from this cache for several reasons; if access is denied to a door when the card is used, after 1 month of inactivity, or if the 2000 limit is reached then as new cards are added to the cache the oldest cards are removed. This attempts to allow an intelligent offline behaviour where the people who use a door recently will still be able to access the door in the event that the door module loses connectivity with the Inception controller.

This option however can force a card to remain stored in the cache, even if it hasn't been used for a long time. This allows important cards like those belonging to managers, emergency services or fire wardens etc to remain working if something happens to the main controller, even if they are rarely used.

Note that cards are not automatically added to the card cache of each SLAM, the card must be presented at each reader of each door and access be successfully granted for the card to be added to the cache of that module.

If enabled, the user will have access to the web interface using the username and password below. The web interface can be accessed on any device with a supported web browser. The device needs to be on the same local network as the Inception Controller or be connected to the Inception Controllers Wi-Fi Hotspot (requires the optional Inception Wireless Adapter). A user can also remotely connect to the web interface using the Skytunnel service, Skytunnel is a hassle free internet based connection method.
option should only be enabled if the user needs to edit programming, control areas, doors, outputs and inputs or to perform administration tasks from the web interface.

You need to assign a web page profile to a user to determine which pages the user can view and interact with. If the chosen web profile allows control of items such as areas, doors or outputs, the user will only be allowed to control the items that you assign in their user permissions. If the user only has permissions to control an area between working hours the same restriction applies to the control of the area from the web interface.
A login name that is used to identify a user and allow access to the web interface. The username can be any length and any combination of letters, numbers and characters.
Each user requires a unique username, you will be unable to save the user programming if a duplicate username is entered.
A secret series of numbers or letters used with the username to allows access to the web interface. The password can be any length and any combination of letters, numbers and characters.

Since Inception is a security product, it is recommended that this password be kept confidential by the user that it has been assigned to. It is also recommended that the password is not saved by the browser that is used to access the web interface. The Inception Controller's web interface can also be accessed over the internet via the Skytunnel architecture, so it is important to have a secure password that can not be guessed.

The Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) enrolment status of the user. 2FA-enrolled users must enter an extra security code from a personal device before they can log into the web interface. Click the link button to open a dialog window to enrol the user for 2FA. NOTE: 2FA must be enabled in Inception's System Settings in order to enrol users for 2FA.
Once a user has been enrolled for 2FA, they will be prompted to enter a security code generated from their personal device in order to confirm the enrolment was successful.

Pressing the X button will delete the existing 2FA configuration for the user. This will cause the user's existing 2FA accounts on smartphone apps to be rendered obsolete, and will require the user to re-enrol for Inception 2FA in order to use it again.

Once a user has initially enrolled for 2FA, the enrolment dialog will no longer be accessible by clicking the link button for security reasons, the 2FA configuration will have to be deleted and re-created in order to re-enrol.

User photos will be displayed with their name in the Who column on the

An image file can be assigned to a user by dragging and dropping the file directly onto the image upload area (if using a PC), or by clicking the upload area and selecting the image that you want to upload. The image's dimensions will be scaled down to 480 x 640px (3:4 aspect ratio) if they exceed this size, so it is recommended that uploaded images meet these conditions for optimal quality when uploaded. The image can be cropped using the Edit button, or deleted with the Remove button.

User expiry is commonly used for contractors or other users who require access to the system for a limited period of time. It allows their user to be configured in advance with credentials and permissions, but only activate at a future time. Similarly, the user can be automatically restricted to prevent their access at a date and time in the future, removing the need for a user to remember to manually edit or delete the user in the future.

Once expired, this user will not be able to interact with the system in any way, preventing access to the web interface and the LCD terminal, credentials will no longer work at readers, their RF fob will not work and they will no longer receive notifications.

If configured, the user will be cancelled when they present their credential to the system for the first time. Depending on configuration, this could be their card or PIN.

This feature is often used where single-use credentials are given to visitors to access the site, which prevents unlimited access in case the card is stolen. The above user expiry options could also be used to manage visitor cards, allowing access for the day only. This option instead allows allows multiple cards to be pre-created (one per user) and handed out as required, without needing to log into the Inception system and update the expiry time for that day.

A profile's priority level is used to create a user management hierarchy, where users are only allowed to create, edit or delete users who have a profile priority level that is lower or equal to their own. Priority levels range from one to five, with a higher number translating to a higher priority.

This options provides a more secure way of protecting the system, preventing for example the person who is assigned the day to day user management from being able to create a new Installer or Administrator user. Similarly, a more senior user with more permissions and abilities can't be edited by a user with a lower priority level.
Web Page Profiles with Priority Five will be able to edit any Web Page Profile's permissions.

This disables Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) which is a server protocol that automatically provides the controller with its IP address. This option should be selected if there is no DHCP server on the connected network or the Inception controller requires a specific IP address.

An IP address is a unique string of numbers separated by full stops that identifies each device using the internet protocol to communicate over a network.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the automatically assigned IP address.

The subnet mask is a binary pattern that is stored in the controller. It is matched with the IP address of a packet to determine which network segment the packet is destined for.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the subnet mask.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the subnet mask.
The default gateway is the router used to forward all traffic that is not addressed to a device within the local network or local subnet.
disabled: an informational only field that displays the default gateway.
enabled: this field is used to manually enter the default gateway.
DNS server is short for domain name system, this is an internet service that translates domain names into IP addresses.

enabled: this field is used to manually enter the DNS server.

Sets the end of line resistance scheme that is used by the system to determine the state of detector inputs. The default setting is 2k2 + 2k2 or 2k2 + 6k8 which supports the resistors supplied with the Inception controller and the Inner Range expansion modules. If you are using a different End of Line resistor configuration, select the one that you are using for the site.

The End of Line configuration such as 2k2 + 6k8 determines the resistance values for the seal and alarm states. The first number 2k2 is the resistance that should represent a sealed input. The sum of both numbers 2k2 + 6k8 = 9k is the resistance for an input in alarm.

If the resistance scheme required is not available, Inner Range may be able to supply an upgrade file for a fee to allow your Inception system to support the custom scheme. Pricing and availability is subject to Inner Range discretion

Determines the duration of time to delay reporting of module AC Fail events to monitoring stations, or to users via notifications. If the AC supply is restored before this delay expires then no message will be sent.

Backup batteries should be fitted to the system to ensure the system can run without an AC supply for an extended period of time. AC supply to the system could be removed for a short period of time, for example, due to a brief power outage or brownout to the site or building maintenance that requires power to be removed for a short period of time. In these cases, power is restored quickly with little drain on the batteries, so the delay prevents the unnecessary notification to the monitoring station of the event. This in turn removes the need for the monitoring station to action the event and follow their response plan of informing the client for the short outage.
, no delay will be used and an AC Fail event will be sent as soon as one is detected.

If enabled, modules and alarm reporting paths will also be tested for any issues when an area arm attempt is made. If a user requested the arm, the attempt would fail if any issues are present and the user would be shown the reason for the failure and given the option to temporarily isolate them and try again. Alternatively, if the system requested the arm, the issues will be temporarily isolated (in the same way as inputs are temporarily isolated) and the arm will succeed. Any health issues isolated by the user or the system in this way will be automatically deisolated when the area disarms.

This ensures that users are made aware of health issues that could compromise the security of the system when securing the premises, allowing them to either fix the issue or make the decision to temporarily override the issue.
The system only monitors module health items that have been configured via the
page. If a health issue will persist for several days, the module can be permanently isolated via the

page. Similar to permanent isolation of inputs, this isolation will persist until a user manually removes it. Alternatively, if the issue will persist for longer or the system should not be monitoring the module for that issue, the module should be edited via the

If a module has its health issues isolated, the system will ignore health issues raised by that module until it is deisolated. Any isolations of health issues are also reported to the monitoring station, ensuring that any potential compromise to the security of the system is known.
If testing of system health is desired on some areas but not others, each area can be configured to ignore health issues via the

request will ignore the Exit Delay part and only enter the Arm Warning phase, arming the area when the phase completes.
A user arm request is considered remote or off-site if it comes from the web interface (i.e the

Arm requests from an LCD terminal, an RF fob, a Reader action, or API integration with a system like a home automation system are all considered on-site.
Determines the duration of time after a module goes offline before the Inception controller updates the state of the modules inputs and peripheral devices.

Rarely, a LAN module may drop offline and re-register itself, coming back online often within one second. This could be caused by LAN signal interference or other factors. In the event that this occurs, this module fail time allows a buffer where the module can restore itself. If the time expires without the module coming back online, the module will be treated as failed and will have the state of its inputs updated and any peripheral devices attached to it set to offline.
It is recommended that you choose a value equal to or greater than your longest module poll time (1 minute by default), as this minimises false detections where the module thinks it is still online.

In this case the detector inputs on the module can not be known to the controller. This could be due to power loss or a lan cabling issue, or it could be due to an intruder intentionally interfering with the LAN.

This could however cause undesirable behaviour (siren responses and alarms being sent to the monitoring station) if sections of the LAN are unreliable
Prevent Siren Retrigger from Same Source

If enabled, an input or module will only be able to trigger the sirens once for an area per arm cycle. Only the retriggering of sirens will be prevented, subsequent activations will still cause alarm events to be reported to a monitoring station if configured.

This option is used for a number of scenarios. If an input is faulty and repeatedly activates, or if it is externally accessible and an intruder can easily activate it, then repeated siren activations could annoy neighbours, cause people to eventually ignore the siren or potentially use up the siren activation limit of the area. With this option enabled however, subsequent activations of an input or module health issue would still be reported to the monitoring station, but the sirens won’t activate. Then, if a second input activates, the sirens will be able to sound and attract attention to the potential break in.
This functionality is also a requirement of AS2201.1 and should be enabled if meeting this standard is desired.

Allows the internal system functionality to be configured with a certain compliance standard in mind. Depending on the option chosen, internal processing logic may change or additional features may become available which are required by the selected standard.
By hiding away functionality that is specific to the compliance modes, it allows the system to remain simpler and easier to use when those standards are not required.

- No additional specific compliance features will be enabled. This is the default option and should be selected for almost all installations, unless compliance with one of the listed standards is required at the site.

- Adds additional processing logic to support the EN50131 standard used in many locations throughout Europe. This adds many things to the system, most of which are internal to the system, while some additional options and features are shown. For example, changes to area alarm processing logic are included, additional Review Events and rules to how they are saved, rules applying to

(installer technicians) are applied and many others. These features, along with additional options and validation, modify the Inception from the original system used in countries around the world to allow compliance with standards required by a number of European countries.

- BS8243 is in general an extension of the EN50131 standard. With this option, in addition to the features and processing logic added to support the EN50131 standard, the extra features and logic required by the BS8243 standard are enabled. On top of the existing EN50131 enhancements, the BS8243 standard primarily adds Confirmation logic to area alarm processing to differentiate between unconfirmed and confirmed intruder and hold up alarm events. Anti-code functionality is also included, allowing the ARC / monitoring station / alarm company to provide a level 2 user with a code to temporarily let them perform installer level actions.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed intruder alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 30 minutes and 1 hour must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Determines the amount of time that can pass after an unconfirmed hold up/duress alarm occurs before the system is reinstated. To be compliant with the BS8243 standard, a time between 8 hours and 20 hours must be selected. However, shorter times are available to aid with testing.

Allows areas to have their confirmation logic grouped together, letting unconfirmed alarms from one area confirm alarms in other areas of the group. This affects both Intruder confirmation and Hold Up confirmation logic.

Inception allows multiple security areas to be defined, letting individual areas be armed and disarmed to provide more flexibility on site design and use. Certain sections of the site can be secured, such as server rooms and other critical areas, while others are freely accessible.

When the BS8243 standard is selected, each area will automatically enable alarm confirmation logic, allowing alarms and other triggers to generate unconfirmed alarms and confirmed alarms in those areas. However, each area is isolated, with alarms in one area not affecting the other areas which may be desirable in some circumstances and not desirable in others when it comes to confirmation logic.

By grouping areas together, it allows an unconfirmed alarm in one area to trigger, then if another unconfirmed alarm occurs in another area within the same group, both areas will confirm their alarms. This allows for a multi-floor building to be able to have individual areas for each floor that can be armed separately, but if an intruder triggers one detector on the first floor and another on the second floor, confirmed alarm events would be activated, instead of multiple unconfirmed alarms. The alternative would be to have one large area covering the whole building, with the only option to have the building either armed or not.

Disarming an area to reinstate it (which restores any unconfirmed or confirmed alarms and stops any confirmation timers), will remove those alarms from the group. For areas that confirmed their unconfirmed alarm based on this other area's alarms, if there are no other alarms in the group, then those areas may restore their confirmed alarm, reverting back to just an unconfirmed alarm and waiting for a second activation.
Multiple groups can be created by selecting which areas should be grouped with the
button, then selecting which confirm group they should belong to.

Entering an unrecognised username in 3 different attempts is distinct and will use the default lockout time.

Specifies the amount of time between mandatory password changes.
