Disallows users from using a PIN to login, removing the 'Switch to PIN Entry' option on the login page and preventing PINs from being used to authenticate with API requests.

If enabled, allows configuration of Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) for users, providing an added level of security when they log into the Inception web interface by requiring them to enter an external code generated by an app running on a smartphone or similar device. The
option below determines which 2FA system will be used for authenticating user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on the

The Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) system that the Inception web interface will utilise for user login. Users can be enrolled for 2FA on a per-user basis from the Manage Users page. For added security, 2FA can also be made compulsory for specific Web Page Profiles, requiring new logged-in users to enrol for 2FA before they are allowed to access the rest of the web interface.

- Configures the controller to request 6-digit Time-based One-time Password (TOTP) codes from users in addition to their username and password. These login codes can be generated by the Google Authenticator mobile app or another TOTP-compatible  2FA app. TOTP codes allow users to log in using 2FA even while the controller or user device cannot connect to the internet.
NOTE: Changing the 2FA method will delete all existing 2FA enrolment data for users and require them to be re-enrolled.

If enabled, the system will have the duress user functionality. This enables a user that is under duress to type in the duress PIN number instead of their own. If the duress user has permission (configured below) they will be granted access as usual, however the system will generate a silent duress alarm event that will be reported to the monitoring station. This event will not generate area sirens or LCD messages.
Sets the PIN number that is used to trigger a duress event from an LCD terminal or keypad reader. This PIN number uses the minimum and maximum PIN lengths values specified on this page in the
section and must be unique compared to all other users in the system.

Sets the permissions provided to a user when triggering a duress event from an LCD Terminal or keypad reader. It is recommended that some permissions are assigned to make it appear that an actual user has logged in, however, it is not recommended that high risk doors and areas are assigned as it would be a PIN number known to many users.

Sets which terminal profile will be applied if the duress user PIN is used to log in to an LCD Terminal. It is recommended that a profile is selected that allows the user access to menus that they would need when under duress and to appear that an actual user logged in. However, the profile shouldn't let them access items like system information, network settings or installer menus as it would be a PIN number known to many users.
Determines how often the Battery Test will run. The frequency is based on a weekly scale range, you can select which day of the week and the what time of the day below.
It is recommended to run the Battery Test regularly to ensure that the in the event of AC failure, each module can be powered from its attached battery.

Once the battery testing is triggered, all modules with a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled will be added to a queue for testing. Modules are tested one at a time so that if there is an AC fail mid test, only part of the system is vulnerable, not all of it.
For a module to be included in the battery test it will need to have a power supply connected, battery connected and battery testing enabled on the
page. The duration of the battery test is set on a per module basis, on the
page. The length of the battery test will depend on the average current consumption of the module and the capacity of the connected battery.

If a battery issue is detected during a battery test, the monitoring station will be notified about it and a warning will appear on the web interface. A module with a failed battery test state must pass a battery test to clear it. This can happen with the next scheduled test, or a test can be manually run on the

Battery tests can abort for a number of reasons, a user can cancel the test, AC was lost on the power supply, a wire was shorted, comms to the module was lost or the module needed to sound sirens. An aborted test is neither a pass or fail and nothing is reported to the monitoring station.
Determines what day of the week the Battery Test will run.
Determines what hour and minute of the day the Battery Test will run.

By default, SIFER readers will be able to read SIFER credentials and ECOLITE credentials. The Multi-Format version of the SIFER reader will also be capable of reading the CSN data of various third party 13.56Mhz credentials.

With this option enabled, the readers will be limited to reading SIFER credentials only. There may be a slight performance improvement with this option as the reader only needs to read one format instead of testing multiple formats. It also offers slightly higher reader security as only the high security SIFER credentials are accepted.

Inception requires a LAN Encryption Key in order to communicate with Ethernet LAN modules on the network. You can either use the default encryption key, or specify a custom passphrase or key to use. All Ethernet LAN modules in your system must be configured with the same encryption key as the controller in order for them to be able to communicate.

If the LAN Encryption Key is changed while Ethernet LAN modules are connected to Inception, Inception will attempt to migrate modules from the old key to the new one. If some modules are failing to connect after changing the LAN encryption key, use the
toolbox button to manually migrate modules from their old key to the new one.
- The default LAN encryption key will be used.
- Allows a passphrase to specified for the LAN encryption key.
- Allows a 32 character hexadecimal key (0-9, A-Z) to be specified for the LAN encryption key.
The passphrase that will be used to derive the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules.

The hexadecimal key that will be used as the LAN Encryption Key for communicating with Ethernet LAN modules. The key must be exactly 32 characters long and can only contain the characters 0-9, A-Z and a-z.

By default, any Intelligent LAN Access Module that is connected will have all entities that can be stored for offline functionality synchronised upon initial connection and any relevant entity changes.

Not all details can be synchronised with full compatibility to the ILAM offline database (such as non-Time Period permissions), so a warning is generated whenever something synchronised does not fully match the Inception system.
With this option enabled, all warnings generated related to ILAM offline database incompatibilities with anything in the Inception system will be suppressed, and will no longer appear.

This page is used to view live and historical events that are created by the Inception Controller. These events can be displayed, filtered and exported.

Review Events are a live and historical log of every event that has occurred on the Inception System. Review events are created every time a scheduled event occurs, a user interacts with a system or even when a programming change is made.
When this page is opened the last 24 hours of events are shown (up to a maximum of 500), new events are dynamically added to the top of the list. If you want to load more events clicking the
button will load an additional 500 events. Clicking the
button will load every event stored on the Inception controller. This is not recommended as displaying this number of events may have an impact on the pages performance.
The displayed events can be filtered to make it easier to find particular events. The
fields are used to filter events to certain dates and times, which is useful if you know what time the event you are looking for occurred. The
field will filter events based on the text you enter. The
filter is used to show or hide events that belong to a particular category, for example, if you were looking for door access events it would be recommended to only show the
The displayed events can be exported as a PDF or CSV file to be printed or used in a different application.
If an incident occurred 1 week ago and you wanted to review the events, click the
button until the events from a week ago are displayed. If there are too many events displayed to find the incident, reduce the number by using one or more filters.
This page is used to configure the users that interact with the Inception Controller and the Inception web interface. This includes their credentials and user permissions.

A user is any person that interacts with the Inception System by either badging a credential, entering a pin number or logging into the web interface. Every user will have one or more credentials and some permissions that determine how and what they can interact with in the system. This page is used to create or manage users, assign credentials and RF Fobs and assign permissions for the Inception Controller and web interface.

Create or select an existing user and assign the credentials required for the user to interact with the system. Credentials are what identify a user and must be unique between all users. Credentials include the PIN number used to request at access keypad readers or log in to the LCD terminal, cards or biometric data for access control and RF remotes for remote control. Also included are the credentials required to log on to the web interface, both via the local network and over the internet via SkyTunnel (if enabled). Web access requires a username, password and a profile assigned, which determines which pages the user can view and how they can interact with those pages.
Permissions determine what areas, doors and outputs a user can access or control on the system and when they are allowed to control them. Permissions are assigned in one of two ways:
Individual items are assigned to each user, this offers a lot of flexibility, however, does not scale very well.

Permission Groups containing many permissions can be created to suit particular roles in a business. Many users who share the same role, for example, all the warehouse staff, could share the same permission group. This option scales well for larger sites.
Individual permissions can also be marked as Deny, allowing

If there are many users that need to be created, you can import multiple users from a CSV file by clicking the

Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view users and not modify them.
It is important to identify how a user should be allowed to interact with the system and only give them permission to do the things they need to do, but nothing more.
This page is used to configure Permission Groups which are a collection of permissions that can be assigned to one or more users that have similar roles.

Permissions can be assigned to users in one of two ways, the first method is to add individual items such as a doors, areas or outputs directly to a user. This method does offer a lot of flexibility however does not scale very well once the site has more than a few users. The preferred method for assigning user’s permission is a permission group. A permission group is a collection of permission rules that can be assigned to multiple users.

Alternatively, you can use a combination of these two methods by assigning the user one or more permission groups based on their role and then allowing or denying additional items on a per user basis. The benefit of this is that it reduces the overall number of permission groups required, with the core permissions that a number of users share forming a permission group and any exceptions to that rule being applied to the individual users. Permission groups can also contain additional permission groups, which may be used when a role in the company requires the same permissions as another, as well as additional permissions. For example, warehouse managers could have a permission group created including the warehouse staff permission group, as well as the other permissions required as a manager. Multiple permission groups could also be combined together in this way.

Permission groups are generally created to suit particular roles on a site such as warehouse staff. The Warehouse Staff permission group would contain all the permissions required for a warehouse worker to access the doors and control the areas and outputs for them to do their job. If a new person was hired in the warehouse, you would simply assign them the Warehouse Staff permission group. The other benefit of using a permission group such as Warehouse Staff, is that if a new door was installed in the warehouse, this door would only need to be added to the permission group once rather than adding it individually to ten warehouse staff members.
Depending on your web page profile you may only be able to view permission groups and not modify them.

When creating permission groups it is recommended to first identify what is common between a number of users, this will generally be role based such as IT Staff, Admin Staff etc. However on some sites it may simply be different level of access such as Basic Access or Management Access. The Basic Access permission group would contain all the doors, areas and outputs needed for every user, you would then provide additional permissions or permission groups to users that have other access requirements.
It is recommended that you clearly name the permission groups based on the role to allow them to be assign to users without needing to look at their contents.
This page is used to create and edit credential templates.

Credential templates are used when assigning credentials (e.g. cards, biometric data) to users. The credential template contains information such as the credential type, the site code and the card format of the cards, if applicable.

field is used by the Inception system to distinguish between different categories of credential data when it is received from a card reader, a fingerprint/iris scanner, or another source. The type of the credential template is taken into account when users attempt to access a door with Dual or Triple Credential Authentication enabled, for example, Dual Credential Authentication requires that at least 2 unique types of credentials are presented at a door reader (e.g. Card type and Fingerprint type) before access is granted.

The site code is a unique facility code that is contained within the data on card type credentials, the site code acts as a second authentication for the credential number. Cards for a site are generally purchased with a single site code, however, multiple purchases of cards can lead to one site using multiple site codes. The card format determines how the data from the card that is badged at a reader is interpreted. This will determine what part of the card data is the credential number and what part of the data is the site code.

When assigning a credential to a user, the first step is to select the credential template. This instructs the system how to interpret that data and what site code to expect. Once a credential template has been added to the user, the credential number is typed in.

If the cards on site use more than one card format or more than one site code a credential template will need to be created for each. Alternatively if the site is only using the raw data from the cards the card format would be set to raw (direct entry).
Without a correctly programmed credential template, no user will be able to access a door.
This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to create and edit remote fob templates.

The Inception Controller supports the use of RF remotes with either the Concept Paradox RF Expander or the Inovonics RF Expander. This will provide users the ability to remotely control a door, area or output from the press of a button.

Remote fob templates are used when assigning remote fobs to users. The remote fob template contains the type of remote and describes what each button on the remote will do. When assigning a remote fob to a user, the remote fob template is selected and the serial number of the remote fob is typed in. This allows multiple users to be given remotes that have the same button behaviour and if any behaviour changes are required it can be edited in one place. Multiple remote fob templates can also be created if different users require different button behaviours.
The Inception System supports the following remotes:
The REM101 is a Paradox single button remote that can be used as a duress pendant or to perform automation.

The REM1, REM2 and REM15 are Paradox four button remotes that can be configured with the main control function using the open and closed padlock buttons. These buttons are used to change the state of a door or area. The unlock button could be used to disarm an area and the lock button to arm. The 2 remaining buttons can be configured to perform one of 3 different actions.
The Inovonics remotes allow custom actions to be configured for each of the buttons on the remote.
Without a correctly programmed remote fob template, the buttons on the remote fob will not do anything.
This page is used to create, edit and control areas.

An area is a collection of inputs that are grouped by their location or purpose. Inputs are assigned to areas and linked with an input type (process group) that determines how the system treats and reacts to the input state changes. Depending on the input type, especially Intruder related ones, certain input state changes may only be processed when the area is armed. The process group determines if sirens or strobes are activated and which alarm reporting message to send, if any.

A user will often arm and disarm an area from an LCD Terminal within that area. This means that the user needs an entry/exit path between the main point of entrance and the LCD Terminal. This is where the detectors along that path won't report alarms or sound sirens during a short entry or exit delay time.

The area can be configured to activate sirens and strobes. The 8 Input Expander can be configured as a Siren Device to drive an internal and external 8-ohm sirens. The Siren Device generates different siren tones based on the process group assigned to the input. In addition to alarm tones, the sirens can also be used to indicate when the area successfully arms, fails to arm, or is about to automatically arm. You can also configure standard outputs as
You can configure an area to automatically arm based on an
. This ensures that rarely used areas are armed more often without needing user interaction, or that areas aren't accidentally left disarmed overnight.

When this page is first opened the list of areas will display the state of each area. As the area changes state the area list dynamically updates. The area state can easily be identified by the text description and colour. The areas can be controlled by clicking the
button in the toolbar, this will open a window that contains control options and a progress display.
Your ability to view and edit areas from this page is based on your Web Page Profile. Your ability to control areas is based on your User Permissions.

The Scheduler is a simple interface for updating door free access times, auto-arming of areas based on a time period and lift floor free access times.

This can be used, for example, to allow free access through a door during working hours, but only once an employee has entered accessed the door first and hence the followed area on the other side of the door has been disarmed.

When naming an input, it is important to use a common naming convention across all inputs to ensure the easy identification of the input purpose and location. Inputs can be configured with the

Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your webpage profile.

This page is used to edit calculated inputs. Calculated inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is controlled by the system itself.

A calculated input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The calculated input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by the system.

Calculated inputs are created automatically under certain circumstances. For example, when a door is created in the system and is assigned a reed or tongue sense input, the Inception controller knows whether that door is open or closed. Two calculated inputs are automatically generated, one that represents when that door is forced (the door opened while locked) and one that represents when that door is open too long (held open past the held time limit and held warning time). These inputs can then be added to areas for monitoring and reporting those events.

The Inception system controls the state of calculated inputs automatically, based on the state of other things in the system. This allows scenarios that would normally require one or more automated actions or complex workarounds to be easily monitored and reported on. For example, the door forced calculated input will only activate if a door opens while in the locked state. This replaces techniques in some other systems where they monitor the reed or tongue input directly and regularly isolate and de-isolate the input on every door unlock.
This page is used to configure calculated inputs such as changing their name and also provides you with the state of the input and the ability to isolate it.
Your ability to view and edit inputs from this page is based on your web page profile.
This page is used by the installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception System.

This page is used to control and edit custom inputs. Custom inputs are virtual/logical inputs that can be used as real hardware inputs throughout the system, but their active/inactive state is instead controlled by automated actions.

A custom input is an input that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom input and the state that it represents can be used in the system as a normal input, being added to areas and assigned an input type, triggering reportable alarm events and activating area sirens. With no physical hardware, the state of the input is instead controlled by automated actions. These actions could be triggered by any state changes within the system, or by a 3rd party system via the 3rd Party Automation Interface, allowing reportable and actionable alarm events to be generated from other systems.

While this feature allows 3rd party system to create reportable alarm events, it should not be used as the method for reporting critical alarms such as fire alarms. It is recommended that a low-level connection is used between the systems involving physical inputs, outputs and end of line resistors for reporting any critical alarms.

This page is used to control and edit custom outputs. Custom outputs are virtual/logical outputs that are primarily used in automation as a state holder that is controlled without a physical output activating.

A custom output is an output that has no physical location or mechanism and exists only within the Inception Controller's logic. The custom output and the state that it represents can then be used to trigger Automated Actions. When programming automation, you may need to use a custom item to trigger some automation or to hold the state of some automation. This could be an output turning on when an input goes into alarm or needing something that is user controllable. It is not recommended to use a physical output as something could eventually be connected to the output or the relay may activate thousands of times a day.
This page is used to create and configure custom outputs and also provides you with the state of the output and the ability to control it.

This page is used to create and edit automated actions to allow additional automation functionality on the Inception system.

The Inception system contains options for many of the commonly used automatic responses of intruder and access control systems. For any custom functionality outside of the core features, automated actions can be used. These would most often be simple automation cases such as turning on the lights when the area is disarmed, but have the flexibility to handle very complicated scenarios.

The automated action is configured in two parts; the trigger condition and the action responses. The trigger condition determines when this automated action is true or false. When the trigger condition changes from false to true, a set of actions can be automatically executed. Similarly, when the trigger condition changes from true to false, a different set of actions can be automatically executed.

You may want the Automated Actions to do the opposite of each other, for example, when the trigger condition is true an output turns on and when false the output turns off. Alternatively, you may only want to setup a single action.
There are no default automated actions, you can add an additional automated action by clicking
This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the automated actions.
This page is used to control and edit doors. Use the Hardware page to add new Doors to your system

An access control door is any electronically controlled obstruction, such as a hinged door, auto door, roller door or boom gate, that restricts a user accessing an area. The door and door related hardware are connected to the Inception Controller or Standard LAN Access Modules (SLAM).
The door is made up of a number of components, including:

- This is the output that controls the electronic lock, when a door is unlocked the output will turn on. The door can be unlocked and locked by a user requesting access with a card or PIN, the web interface, or by pressing a REX (Request to Exit) button. The door can also be configured to automatically unlock and lock based on schedules or the state of the area on one side of the door.

- SIFER/OSDP or Wiegand readers are installed on one side or both sides of a door. The readers provide the controller with card or PIN information to identify a user who is requesting access. If they have permission to access the door, the door can unlock.

inputs are usually wired to buttons or detectors that, if pressed, will cause the door to briefly unlock. Often placed on the side of the door where access control is not restricted, allowing the door to unlock to exit the door without presenting a credential or creating a door forced alarm.
- These inputs allow the system to know if the door is open/unsecured or closed securely. This can be used with the door's lock state to identify DOTL (Door Open Too Long) or Door Forced states.

- DOTL (Door Open Too Long) is a logical state for a door which occurs when the door has been unlocked and opened and the system has tried to lock the door, however the door has not been closed within the
configured on this page. The DOTL input goes into the alarm state once the configured
expire without the door closing securely. Once triggered, the DOTL input seals when the door is closed.

- Door Forced is a logical state for a door which occurs when a door is opened without the door being unlocked first. When this state occurs the door forced input will go into alarm. Once triggered, the Door Forced input will seal when the door closes or is unlocked.

If doors are configured with inside and outside areas, numerous features can be used. User locations can update with access requests, the door can follow the area state to prevent unlocking when the area is armed, readers linked to the door can provide audible and visual feedback for area state changes and the readers can also be used to arm the area.
The door is created and configured in the
page. This page provides the same options as the

page, as well as some additional advanced options to configure the operation of the door. In addition to naming and configuring the doors, this page provides the state and control of doors, allowing you to see live state changes to any configuration changes you make. If your web page profile and user permissions allow, you can click on the control button to unlock, lock, override and grant access to the selected door.
This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to edit readers. Readers must be configured from the
page in order for them to appear on this page.

The reader is the access point that a user interacts with to unlock a door. Depending on the door there may be a reader on one side or on both sides of the door. The reader is usually configured and associated with a door in the configuration hardware menu. This page provides additional reader specific options to configure the operation of the reader.

A large number of options on this page are not editable as they are determined by the configuration hardware menu. The options displayed on this page will vary based on the type of reader connected, the common option across all readers is a three badge action. The three badge action is the action that will occur when a user presents a card three times in quick succession to the reader. This action can be set to arm the area on the other side of the door to where this reader is located, or arm all of the areas in the system. Please note that if the
Arm the Area on the Other Side of the Door
option is selected the door programming requires an area to be specified.

When using SIFER or OSDP readers, they can be configured to provide door or area feedback, including showing state information via the status LED. For SIFER readers this is the small LED, for OSDP readers it is the main LED. SIFER readers also include options to change the volume of the beeps produced by the SIFER reader and the brightness of the LEDs or the colour to display to customize the appearance if not showing state feedback.
This page is used by the Installer during the initial installation and ongoing maintenance of the Inception system.
This page is used to enrol and configure hardware modules.

The hardware page displays all the unconfigured and configured modules on Inception System. A hardware module is any expansion module that connects to the Inception controller by the RS-485 LAN to provide additional inputs, outputs, doors or readers. For the devices to work correctly they must first be configured.

When configuring a module, you will be stepped through multiple sections of programming. These steps will depend on the type of module that is being configured, for example, a reader module will have reader and door programming options as well as inputs and outputs, however, an input expander will only have options for inputs and outputs.
A module can be configured by selecting it and clicking the

What you able to do on this page will depend on your web page profile, in some cases, you may only be able to view the hardware modules and not edit them.
This page is used to create and edit lift floors.

A lift floor is a destination that a button in a lift car could take a user to. A lift floor can either span multiple lift cars or be limited to a single lift car. A floor that spans multiple lift cars usually contains an area or facilities that are required by many users such as a basement carpark. When a lift floor is only accessed by a single lift car it is usally beacause the lift car serves a particular purpose, such as a service lift or one that provides access to restricted levels.

Users are granted permission to access lift floors, so that when they present a card or PIN at a reader within a lift car, the buttons that would take them to the floors they can access would be enabled by Inception. Lift floors can also be in one of three states,

.  A floor in free access will cause all of the lift cars that can access that floor to enable the button for that floor, allowing any user to select that floor without needing to present a card. A secured floor requires a card to be presented in a lift car before a button linked to the floor would be enabled. A locked out floor will prevent a button linked to the floor from enabling, even if a user with permission to the floor presents their card.
Lift floors are secured by default, however a time period and an area can be configured to schedule when free access to a floor is allowed. When the time period assigned to the
is valid, the floor will be in free access and if invalid, the floor will be secure. This can be used with the
option, causing the floor to be in free access only if the time period is valid and the
is disarmed, otherwise the floor is secure. This can allow, for example, free access to a floor during working hours but only once an employee has entered the area and disarmed the area.

There doesn’t need to be one lift floor created per building floor though, in some cases there may be multiple lift floors created for the same physical floor in a building, for example if the lift car has two doors and there are different buttons for 7A and 7B. Two lift floors can then be created with different areas and permissions assigned to each side of the floor. Alternatively, if a particular lift car needs its access to be restricted to only certain users, for example a service elevator, there could be normal floors created for the other cars, along with another set of service floors for the service lift car, with only maintenance workers having permission to those floors.

You need only create lift floors for the levels in a building where you wish to restrict users access. If there are lift car buttons and floors that Inception does not need to control access to, then they do not require a lift floor.

If using the lift integration functionality for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift floors could be created to represent the inside of the locker or parking spot, or whatever destination that is being protected by an output. Users could then be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit lift cars.

A lift car represents a physical lift car or button panel that the user interacts with. Lift cars require hardware outputs in the Inception system to be wired to the lift button panel so they can enable and disable whether the lift button works. Inputs can also be wired into the button to indicate when the button has been pressed. Using this information and ability in addition to a credential reader, allows Inception to control which floor buttons a user is allowed to press when they present their card, biometric, or PIN credential.
Lift cars are made up of a number of components, including:

- Multiple readers can be configured for a single lift car, along with the number of credential types that are required for a user to be identified (Single, Dual or Triple Credential Authentication). This allows, for example, readers to be mounted at different heights for accessibility, multiple readers to be used for different credential types or a separate reader and keypad for card and PIN entry.
- Represents each physical lift button that Inception is able to control. Requires both a
for the button's destination and the output that has been wired into the lift button. Optionally, an input can be specified that would indicate when the button has been pressed.
- These are the outputs that can be used to give visual or audible feedback to the user in the lift. A number of outputs can be specified along with how they should behave.
- Configures how long to enable the buttons for once a user presents their credential. Includes an optional extended enable time if the user presenting their credential has the
The inputs, outputs and readers required by the lift car must be created separately using the

A lift car should be created for the physical button panel that it controls. This could mean, for example, that a destination panel in the lobby would also be treated as a lift car. Also, if there are two readers and two button panels within the same lift car, then two separate lift car items should be created so the button panels can be controlled individually.

If the lift integration functionality is being used for a reason other than lift car control, such as lockers, garages etc, then lift cars would link the reader of the system to the outputs that are being controlled. The readers would still be where the user presents their credential, however a button would instead represent a locker door or a garage roller door that can be controlled by an output. The lift floor would be the inside of the locker or the parking spot and the users could be given permission to access that lift floor.
This page is used to create and edit storage units.

To protect the storage units, they can contain motion or door position detectors to monitor unauthorized access to the space. If unauthorized access is detected, the unit enters an alarm state which can sound sirens and be reported to a monitoring station.
To monitor a storage unit, when a unit is created, a

input type, which will cause local sirens if that area is disarmed and sirens and a reported alarm event if that area is armed. This allows staff to control whether alarm events should be reported for the unit alarms, for example, leaving the area disarmed while staff are on site during the day, and arming at night for after-hours customer access.

option will also turn on when the unit unlocks, which could be used to control unit lighting, a door lock, etc. If a unit is not being rented, it can be marked as
allowing free access to the unit.
The unit will enter a Tamper state if any of the inputs within the unit are tampered with.

for a block, their unit can be automatically unlocked. Similary, when they leave the block via an

time to be configured which alerts when a unit has been unlocked for too long, and an

Users are granted permission to control storage units, letting them unlock and secure the unit and control the vacancy state. In addition, if a user has

permission to a unit, it will automatically unlock as they enter the Block and automatically secure when they leave. The granular permissions allow customers to be able to conveniently access the unit or units they are renting, while allowing staff users full control over all the units.

User permissions to units and storage unit state can both be managed via the REST API, allowing integrations to automatically create users and give them permission to units, as well as controlling those units and their vacancy.
This page is used to create and edit storage blocks.

A storage block represents a group of storage units. This could represent an entire site with 100s of units, a single building of units in a multi-building site, a single section of a building, and so on. There can be as many blocks as required in a system.
Storage blocks are made up of a number of components, including:

- Multiple readers can be configured for a block's entry. These could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an entry gate, the entry door of a building, or so on. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have
permission to control to unlock, if that unit is in this block.

- Multiple readers can be configured for a block's exit. Similar to Entry Readers, these could be dedicated readers or the readers that control existing access control points like an exit gates or building exit door. A user presenting a credential at one of these readers will cause any Units that they have
permission to control to be secured, if that unit is in this block.
- Any outputs selected will turn on if any unit in the block is unlocked. This can allow building or section lighting to turn on only if any units in that building/section are unlocked.
- These are the units that belong in this block. If required, units can belong to more than one block.
- Configures how long to units in this block will wait before automatically re-securing after their door was opened and then closed.
- Configures how long to units in this block will wait before entering the Unlocked Too Long state.
The outputs and readers required by the storage block must be created separately using the

A storage block should represent a logical set of storage units with clear entry and exit points controlled with access control. For simple sites, there may be one block for the entire site with entry and exit gate readers used for automatic unit control in the block. Blocks could also represent a building full of units, and the entry and exit readers for that building would be used by the block.

Blocks serve a secondary feature though, providing summary states of their units for use with automation. Many smaller blocks could be created that all use the same entry and exit readers, but only control a smaller set of blocks. This would allow more granular section lighting or other automation to be easily managed.

Alternatively, to better manage common settings, a whole site block could exist with entry and exit readers to control all of the units, set common resecure and unlocked too long times, and so on. Additional blocks can then be created just for local building automation. Those additional blocks wouldn't have readers configured, and would just contain the units in a section of the building and the outputs to control lighting in that section. If using this approach, be sure to set the Unit Unlocked Too Long and Unit Auto-Resecure times to None to not override the values in the main Block.

This page is used to create and edit connections that allow the Inception system to handle text-based 2-way communications with other systems. These connections can be used to trigger certain actions when specific messages are received from the other system, or send messages to the other system when events occur in the Inception system.

Inception's Connections are intended to act as low-level, generic text based communication handlers which enable integration with other systems through sending and receiving short text messages over a network, serial or HTTP connection.

Serial Connections can be configured to connect to any serial device that has been previously connected to the Inception system. You can choose to either connect to the first plugged in and available serial device, or pick one of the devices from a list. The baud rate, parity bit, number of data bits and number of stop bits are all configurable for serial connections.

TCP Connections can be configured to act as a server and listen for incoming connections on a specified port on the Inception device, or to act as a client and establish a connection to a specified server given an IP address and port number.

Connections work by opening a connection to an IP address/port number, and holding that connection open indefinitely while sending text messages through to the other system. They are also capable of bidirectional communication through receiving text messages back from the other system's server, if the server supports that functionality. If the TCP connection fails due to network problems or configuration issues, the
connection will continually attempt to reconnect to the server until it is successful.
Connections work by continuously listening for incoming connections on a specific port and accepting clients, and accepting all incoming text messages from any of those connected clients. Only one

connection can be listening on a single port at a given time; attempting to have more than one connection listening on the same port will result in the first connection successfully listening on that port and the others failing to initialise. The
connection is also capable of sending text messages to the connected clients if an action uses the server connection as a target. Sending a text message through the

connection will forward that message to all TCP clients currently connected to the server (if no clients are connected, no message will be sent), which allows for bidirectional communication with clients if they support this functionality.
If bi-directional communication over TCP is desired, both the

connection types are capable of both sending and receiving messages over the one connection; however, it is generally more consistent and reliable to use a dedicated connection for each responsibility, i.e. using a dedicated client connection solely for sending messages and a dedicated server connection solely for receiving messages, if the other system supports this configuration.

Connections work by sending a HTTP GET request to a URL on a web server, with optional authentication. This type of connection is best suited for communicating with systems that expose a HTTP API, for example, a camera that can be made to perform pan & zoom actions when it receives requests for certain URLs. When the HTTP connection sends a
, it works by appending the message text to the URL Path specified in the connection details, and performing a GET request aimed at that URL. For example, if the URL Path is
and the message being sent is

response from the server, or no response at all, the connection status will reflect this by changing to

authentication for sending requests to systems that require user authorisation.
authentication is the simplest and most common type, and it only requires a username & password to be set.

authentication is less commonly used but can be more secure, as it only sends hashed information. Digest authentication requires that a username, password, and domain is specified. Check the requirements of the target system's HTTP API to find out what type of authentication should be used for the Connection, if any.

If a Connection is repeatedly failing or messages are not being sent/received properly, enabling

page. For example, if the review messages show that only partial messages are being received, there may be a problem with the Connection's framing strings.

Connections can be optionally configured to have framing characters to denote the start or end of a message being sent or received. These framing characters also support escape sequences to aid in specifying special characters like newline or null characters. For example, setting the suffix for received messages to '\
\
' would require the other system to end all messages with a carriage return + line feed character sequence to signify that the message ends before those characters.
Incoming messages received through Connections can be used to trigger actions in the Inception system by configuring an Automated Action (
) and using the Connection item as part of a trigger condition. They can also be used to send messages when an Automated Action is triggered, by adding a
response to the Automated Action. Text messages in trigger conditions and action responses can also contain any of the escape sequences that the framing prefixes/suffixes support.
Your web page profile will determine if you can create or modify the items on this page.
This page is used to create and edit counters to allow advanced automation events when used in conjunction with automated actions.

Counters are items that contain an integer number value for their internal state, which can be modified manually or by events in the system. They have no additional properties besides an optional minimum and maximum limit value; the counter's functionality consists entirely of its state value, which can be viewed and controlled on the

Counters are primarily intended for usage in combination with automated actions, to allow advanced automation functionality to be configured, such as triggering an action once a certain number of events have occurred, e.g. an input activation counter or reader badge counter. Counter state values are persisted across system power cycles.
The counter's value can be restricted to a certain range by using the

fields. Attempting to set a counter's value outside this range will cause it to be clamped to the nearest min or max limit value. When setting the minimum and maximum allowed values for a counter, the minimum value cannot be larger than the maximum value, and vice versa.
You can control counters on this page by clicking the
button in the toolbox in the top-right corner of the page. The control dialog allows you to increment, decrement and manually set values for counters.

Counters can be used in conjunction with automated actions in 2 ways: as a trigger condition, and as an action response. As a trigger condition, counters can be used to trigger actions when their value becomes less than, greater than, or equal to a certain value. As an action response, the automated action can increment or decrement the counter's value by a specific number, or set the counter's value to a specific number.
This page should only be used by the installer during the setup or ongoing maintenance of the system. Your web page profile will determine if you can view or view and edit the counters.
This page is used to view and control the state of areas. To control an area you must have the appropriate user permissions and web page profile.
This page will dynamically display the state of areas on the Inception Controller. This page also allows you to arm and disarm areas remotely via web interface.

When this page is first opened it will display the current state of all the areas and the page will dynamically update as the areas change state. The area state can easily be identified by the colour of the area and the text description in the
The areas can be controlled in three different ways:
button for each area will arm or disarm the area based on its current state.

The areas that you are able to control will be based on your user permissions which applies any time or control restrictions. Your web page profile will also determine if you are restricted to only view or view and control the areas.
